mnemonics.title: Hypervisor Types
mnemonics.content: Type 1 = 'First' (Bare Metal) - Runs Directly; Type 2 = 'Second' (Hosted) - Runs on OS

mnemonics.title: Virtualization Benefits
mnemonics.content: S.A.F.E.R. = Savings, Agility, Flexibility, Efficiency, Reliability

mnemonics.title: VM Components
mnemonics.content: V.I.P.S. = Virtual Hardware, ISO File, Processor allocation, Storage allocation

acronyms.usage: Isolated software container with its own OS and applications
Technology for hosting desktop environments on central servers
Standard format for packaging virtual appliances
Open standard for packaging and distributing virtual machines
Memory architecture affecting VM performance in capacity planning
Interface for connecting storage devices to virtualized servers

visualTips.visualization: Draw a rectangle (physical server) with the hypervisor on top, then multiple smaller rectangles (VMs) stacked side by side, each containing its own OS. Type 1 shows hypervisor directly on hardware; Type 2 shows hypervisor on host OS.
Imagine three separate buckets (servers) being poured into one large swimming pool (resource pool), then distributed to various containers (VMs) as needed.
Think of moving a running program from one computer to another without closing it - like carrying a running television from one room to another without turning it off.
VM = house with own kitchen; Container = apartment with shared kitchen. Both isolated but different resource sharing levels.

analogies.analogy: Like apartment building - one physical building (hardware) divided into multiple separate living spaces (VMs), each with its own utilities but sharing common infrastructure.
Like a building manager - coordinates and allocates resources (elevator, water, electricity) among all apartments (VMs) in the building.
Like shipping containers - standardized packages that can be easily moved, shared resources but isolated contents, unlike full houses (VMs).
Like airlines overselling seats - counting on the fact that not all passengers will show up, but risking delays if everyone arrives.
